name: Las Médulas
description: Largest open-cast gold mine in the Roman Empire, using ruina montium (hushing) technique. Produced an estimated 5 tonnes of gold.

name: Río Tinto
description: Massive polymetallic mining complex in Hispania Baetica, producing silver, copper, and gold since pre-Roman times.

name: Río Tinto Copper Works
description: Copper extraction at Río Tinto complex, one of the largest sources of copper in the ancient world.

name: Carthago Nova Silver Mines
description: Silver mines near Carthago Nova (modern Cartagena), captured from the Carthaginians by Scipio Africanus. Employed 40,000 workers according to Polybius.

name: Mazarrón Mines
description: Silver and lead mining district in southeastern Hispania, part of the Cartagena mining region.

name: Castulo Mines
description: Major silver and lead mining district near Castulo in the upper Guadalquivir valley, Hispania Tarraconensis.

name: Aljustrel (Vipasca)
description: Copper and silver mines in Lusitania. Source of the Vipasca tablets, which detail Roman mining law and administration.

name: Três Minas
description: Large Roman gold mine in northern Lusitania employing hushing and deep underground extraction methods.

name: Valongo Gold Mines
description: Gold mining complex near modern Porto in Lusitania, with extensive Roman galleries.

name: Sotiel Coronada
description: Copper and silver mine in the Iberian Pyrite Belt of Hispania Baetica.

name: Laurion Silver Mines
description: Famous Athenian silver mines that financed the Greek fleet at Salamis. Continued under Roman rule but at diminished output.

name: Laurion Lead Workings
description: Lead was a major byproduct of silver extraction at Laurion, used extensively for pipes and construction.

name: Roșia Montană (Alburnus Maior)
description: Principal gold mine of Roman Dacia, with extensive underground galleries. Wax tablets found here document mining contracts.

name: Ampelum Gold District
description: Administrative center for the Dacian gold mines, seat of the procurator aurariarum. Modern Zlatna, whose name means 'golden'.

name: Brad Gold Mines
description: Gold mining complex in the Metaliferi Mountains of Dacia, part of the Apuseni gold district.

name: Baia de Arieș Gold Mine
description: Roman gold mine in western Dacia, part of the extensive Apuseni Mountains gold district.

name: Cornwall Tin Mines
description: Ancient tin mining region in southwestern Britannia, source of tin for bronze production since the Bronze Age. Known to Phoenician traders.

name: Penwith Tin Streams
description: Alluvial tin streaming operations in the Penwith peninsula, westernmost Cornwall.

name: Mendip Lead Mines
description: Rapidly exploited after the Roman conquest of Britain. Lead pigs stamped 'EX ARG' indicate silver extraction from lead ore.

name: Charterhouse-on-Mendip
description: Small Roman mining settlement on Mendip, where silver was extracted from galena ore. Lead pigs found across Britain stamped with this origin.

name: Derbyshire Lead Mines (Lutudarum)
description: Major lead mining district in the Peak District of Britannia, identified as Lutudarum from inscribed lead pigs.

name: Flintshire Lead Mines
description: Lead mining district in northeastern Wales, with stamped lead pigs attesting to Roman exploitation.

name: Dolaucothi Gold Mines
description: Only known Roman gold mine in Britain, using aqueducts and hushing to extract gold. A fort was built nearby to protect operations.

name: Swaledale Lead Mines
description: Lead mining in the Yorkshire Dales under Roman administration, with evidence of smelting sites.

name: Mons Porphyrites
description: Imperial porphyry quarry in the Eastern Desert of Egypt. Only known source of imperial purple porphyry, reserved for emperors.

name: Mons Claudianus
description: Imperial granite quarry producing grey granodiorite. Columns for the Pantheon and Trajan's Forum were quarried here.

name: Aswan Granite Quarries (Syene)
description: Ancient red granite quarries used since pharaonic times, continuing under Roman rule. Source of monolithic obelisks and columns.

name: Wadi Hammamat Quarries
description: Bekhen stone (greywacke/siltstone) quarry in the Eastern Desert, used since predynastic times through the Roman period.

name: Luna/Carrara Marble Quarries
description: Premier white marble quarries of the Roman Empire, supplying marble for the buildings of Augustan and later Rome. Augustus boasted of turning Rome from brick to marble.

name: Pentelikon Marble Quarries
description: Source of the fine white marble used for the Parthenon and many Roman-period buildings in Athens.

name: Paros Marble Quarries
description: Source of lychnites, a translucent white marble prized for sculpture. Used for the Venus de Milo and many Roman copies of Greek statues.

name: Thasos Marble Quarries
description: White and grey marble quarries on the island of Thasos, extensively exported throughout the Roman Mediterranean.

name: Proconnesos Marble Quarries
description: Major marble quarry island in the Sea of Marmara, becoming the dominant source of marble in Late Antiquity for Constantinople.

name: Docimium Marble Quarries
description: Source of pavonazzetto (Phrygian purple-veined marble), highly prized in Rome. Located near Docimium in Phrygia.

name: Simitthu/Chemtou Marble Quarries
description: Source of giallo antico (Numidian yellow marble), one of the most valued decorative stones in Rome. Imperial quarry from Augustus onward.

name: Djebel Filfila Quarries
description: White marble quarries near Hippo Regius in Numidia, supplying marble for North African Roman cities.

name: Bou Hanifia Quarries
description: Onyx marble quarries in Mauretania Caesariensis, producing decorative stone for North African and Italian buildings.

name: Skouriotissa Copper Mine
description: Ancient copper mine on Cyprus, the island that gave copper its name (cyprium). Massive slag heaps attest to Roman-era production.

name: Tamassus Copper Mine
description: One of the oldest copper mining centers on Cyprus, mentioned by ancient authors. City prospered from copper trade.

name: Soli Copper Mine
description: Copper mining center near the ancient city of Soli on northern Cyprus, with Roman-era galleries and smelting facilities.

name: Noricum Iron Mines (Hüttenberg)
description: Famous Noric iron/steel production center. Noric steel (ferrum Noricum) was renowned for sword blades throughout the Roman world.

name: Magdalensberg Iron Works
description: Major iron production and trading center on the Magdalensberg, key emporium for Noric iron before the Roman conquest.

name: Elba Iron Mines
description: Major iron ore source since Etruscan times, supplying iron for central Italy. Ore was shipped to Populonia for smelting.

name: Bilbilis Iron Mines
description: Iron mining and metalworking center in Hispania Tarraconensis. Martial, the poet born here, praised the quality of Bilbilis steel.

name: Wealden Iron Mines
description: Major iron production region in southeastern Britannia, with Roman-era bloomeries and possibly state-controlled operations.

name: Forest of Dean Iron Mines
description: Iron mining and smelting district in western Britannia, producing iron for military and civilian use.

name: Sierra Menera Iron Mines
description: Extensive iron deposits in eastern Hispania, exploited from pre-Roman through Roman times.

name: Cantabrian Iron Mines
description: Iron deposits exploited after Augustus's Cantabrian Wars. Part of the mineral wealth of northern Hispania.

name: Montagne Noire Iron Mines
description: Iron mining district in southern Gallia Narbonensis, with extensive Roman-era slag heaps.

name: Sardinian Lead Mines (Iglesiente)
description: Lead and silver mining district in southwestern Sardinia, exploited from the Carthaginian period through Roman times.

name: Linares Silver Mines
description: Silver-lead mining district in the Sierra Morena of Hispania, one of the major sources of Roman silver coinage metal.

name: Jales Gold Mine
description: Gold mine in northern Lusitania with well-preserved Roman underground workings and water-management systems.

name: Asturian Gold Mines
description: Extensive gold mining district in Asturia, developed after the Cantabrian Wars. Pliny described the mining techniques used here.

name: Gallaecian Gold Mines
description: Gold mining region in Gallaecia (modern Galicia), part of the northwest Iberian gold district exploited under Augustus.

name: Estremoz Marble Quarries
description: White and pink marble quarries in Lusitania, widely used in the monumental architecture of Roman cities in Hispania.

name: Almadén de la Plata Quarries
description: Marble quarries in Hispania Baetica supplying polychrome marble for buildings in Italica and other Baetican cities.

name: Karystos Marble Quarries
description: Source of cipollino marble (marmor Carystium), a green-veined marble widely used for columns in Rome and across the empire.

name: Skyros Marble Quarries
description: Polychrome marble quarries producing breccia-like decorative stone used in elite Roman architecture.

name: Göktepe Marble Quarries
description: Recently identified quarry in Caria producing black-and-white marble used for Roman imperial portraits.

name: Aphrodisias Marble Quarries
description: Local marble quarries supplying the famous sculpture workshops of Aphrodisias in Caria.

name: Ephesos Area Marble Quarries
description: Multiple marble quarries supplying the monumental architecture of Roman Ephesos.

name: Thasos Gold Mines
description: Ancient gold mines on Thasos exploited from Archaic times, with continued Roman-era extraction.

name: Mt Pangaeum Gold Mines
description: Famous gold and silver mines in Thrace, formerly exploited by Philip II of Macedon and continuing into Roman times.

name: Mt Pangaeum Silver Mines
description: Silver mines on Mount Pangaeum in Thrace that financed Philip II's army. Reduced output under Roman administration.

name: Sifnos Silver Mines
description: Cycladic silver and gold mines famous in the Archaic period, with diminished Roman-era activity.

name: Dalmatian Iron Mines
description: Iron mining in the interior of Dalmatia, supporting military equipment production for the Danube frontier legions.

name: Moesian Gold Mines
description: Gold mining in Moesia Superior, expanded after Trajan's Dacian Wars brought the entire region under firm Roman control.

name: Srebrenica Silver Mines
description: Silver mining district in the Dinaric Alps, part of the Dalmatian-Pannonian mining region. Town name means 'silver place'.

name: Rudnik Lead Mines
description: Lead and silver mining in central Moesia Superior, with evidence of Roman mine administration.

name: Timacum Minus Copper Mines
description: Copper mining center in Moesia Superior near the military camp of Timacum Minus.

name: Almadén (Sisapo) Cinnabar Mine
description: World's largest cinnabar (mercury sulfide) deposit, known as Sisapo. Supplied vermillion pigment to Rome under sealed contracts described by Pliny.

name: Krokeai Porphyry Quarries
description: Source of lapis lacedaemonius (green porphyry), a decorative stone used in elite Roman buildings.

name: Teos Marble Quarries
description: Source of africano marble (marmor Luculleum), a dark red-purple breccia highly valued by the Roman elite.

name: Hatnub Alabaster Quarries
description: Egyptian travertine (calcite alabaster) quarry used since Old Kingdom times, continuing sporadically under Roman rule.

name: Tura Limestone Quarries
description: Fine white limestone quarries near Memphis, used for pyramid casing stones and continuing to supply building stone in Roman Egypt.

name: Bath Limestone Quarries
description: Oolitic limestone quarries supplying building stone for Aquae Sulis (Bath) and surrounding Roman villas in Britannia.

name: Portland Limestone Quarries
description: Limestone quarrying on the Isle of Portland in Britannia, with evidence of Roman-era extraction.

name: Heiligenberg Sandstone Quarries
description: Sandstone quarries in Germania Superior supplying building stone for Roman military installations and civilian settlements along the Rhine.

name: Río Tinto Gold Workings
description: Gold extraction at the Río Tinto polymetallic complex using fire-setting and water-powered ore crushing.

name: Armorican Tin Mines
description: Tin mining in the Armorican peninsula (modern Brittany), supplementing Cornish tin supplies.

name: Galician Tin Mines
description: Alluvial tin deposits in Gallaecia (northwestern Hispania), exploited alongside gold extraction.

name: Wadi Faynan Copper Mines
description: Ancient copper mining district in the Arabah Valley (modern Jordan), with extensive Roman-era operations including convict labor.

name: Timna Copper Mines
description: Ancient copper mines in the southern Arabah, with evidence of Roman-era reworking of earlier Egyptian and Nabataean operations.

name: Ergani Maden Copper Mine
description: Major copper deposit in southeastern Anatolia, one of the ancient Near East's most important copper sources, active in the Roman period.

name: Baalbek Limestone Quarries
description: Quarries producing the enormous limestone blocks used in the Temple of Jupiter at Heliopolis, including stones weighing over 800 tonnes.

name: Bekaa Valley Quarries
description: Limestone quarries in the Bekaa Valley of Syria, supplying building stone for Heliopolis and other Roman cities.

name: Assos Sarcophagus Quarries
description: Quarries producing lapis sarcophagus, a type of stone said to consume flesh rapidly, giving sarcophagi their name.

name: Pannonian Iron Mines
description: Iron mining district in western Pannonia, part of the eastern Alpine iron production zone.

name: Berry Iron District
description: Iron mining and smelting center in central Gaul (Bituriges Cubi territory), producing iron on an industrial scale.

name: Lorraine Iron Mines
description: Iron mining in northeastern Gaul (Belgica), with evidence of extensive Roman-era smelting operations.

name: Cerro Muriano Copper Mine
description: Copper mine north of Corduba in Hispania Baetica, with substantial Roman-era underground workings.

name: Djebba Lead Mines
description: Lead mining in the Medjerda valley of Africa Proconsularis, supplying lead for the North African provinces.

name: Funtana Raminosa Copper Mine
description: Copper mine in central Sardinia with evidence of both Punic and Roman exploitation.

name: Mátra Mountains Gold Mines
description: Gold mining in the Mátra range of Pannonia, a secondary gold source for the Danubian provinces.

name: Gebel el-Silsila Sandstone Quarries
description: Massive sandstone quarries on the Nile between Aswan and Luxor, used since the New Kingdom and continuing in Roman Egypt.

name: Leptis Magna Quarries
description: Local limestone quarries supplying building stone for Leptis Magna's monumental Roman architecture.

name: Naxos Marble Quarries
description: Coarse-grained marble quarries on Naxos, famous for Archaic sculpture and continuing to supply stone in Roman times.

name: Ecton Copper Mines
description: Bronze Age and Roman-era copper mining in the Peak District of Britannia.

name: Great Orme Copper Mines
description: Prehistoric and Roman-era copper mine on the Great Orme headland in northern Wales.

name: Parys Mountain Copper Mine
description: Copper mine on Anglesey with evidence of exploitation from the Bronze Age through the Roman period.

name: Mitterberg Copper Mines
description: Ancient copper mining center in the Austrian Alps, with continued but diminished activity into the early Roman period.

name: Cartagena Lead Mines
description: Lead mining associated with the silver extraction at Carthago Nova, producing large quantities of lead as a byproduct.

name: Clunia Area Quarries
description: Limestone quarries supplying building stone for the conventus capital of Clunia in Hispania Tarraconensis.

name: Saint-Béat Marble Quarries
description: White and grey marble quarries in the Pyrenees, supplying marble for Gallic and Hispanic Roman cities.

name: Laurion Lead-Zinc Mines
description: Reworking of Laurion mine tailings and deeper deposits for lead and zinc in the Roman period.

name: Macedonian Iron Mines
description: Iron mining in the mountains of Macedonia, supplying the military workshops of the Balkan provinces.

name: Troad Gold Mines
description: Gold deposits in the Troad region of Asia Minor, exploited from pre-Roman times.

name: Tmolus Gold Mines
description: Gold deposits on Mt Tmolus in Lydia, the region where coinage was invented. Source of Lydian electrum and later Roman gold.

name: Pontic Iron Mines (Chalybes)
description: Iron mines in the land of the Chalybes on the Black Sea coast, legendary inventors of iron smelting. Active through Roman times.

name: Cappadocian Silver Mines
description: Silver deposits in Cappadocia supplying local and regional coin production under Roman administration.

name: Phaeno Copper Mines
description: Copper mines in the Wadi Araba (Phaeno), used as a place of condemnation to hard labor (damnatio ad metalla) for early Christians.

name: Beni Hasan Limestone Quarries
description: Limestone quarries in Middle Egypt near Hermopolis, used from pharaonic through Roman times.

name: Troezen Quarries
description: Limestone quarries on the Argolid peninsula, supplying building stone for Roman Peloponnesian cities.

name: Tivoli Travertine Quarries
description: Travertine quarries near Tibur supplying Rome's most important structural stone, used for the Colosseum and numerous temples.

name: Zaghouan Lead Mines
description: Lead mining in the hills of Africa Proconsularis, near the source of the great Carthage aqueduct.

name: Aznalcóllar Copper Mine
description: Copper mining site in the Iberian Pyrite Belt near ancient Hispalis (Seville).

name: Limousin Gold Mines
description: Gold mining district in the Massif Central of Gaul, with evidence of Roman-era hydraulic mining.

name: Aurisina Limestone Quarries
description: Fine limestone quarries near Aquileia, supplying building stone for the major Roman port city.

name: Brač Island Limestone Quarries
description: White limestone quarries on Brač island, supplying building stone for Diocletian's Palace at Spalatum (Split) and other Dalmatian cities.

name: Cerro del Plomo Copper Mine
description: Copper mining installation in the Sierra Morena, part of the mineral-rich Cordoba mining district.

name: Clogau Gold Mine
description: Gold mine in the Dolgellau gold belt of Wales, with evidence of Roman-era exploitation.

name: Melle Silver-Lead Mines
description: Silver-bearing lead deposits in western Gaul, becoming important for Merovingian coinage but with Roman-era origins.

name: Aachen Area Lead Mines
description: Lead-zinc mining in the Eifel region of Germania Inferior, with Roman smelting installations.

name: Felsberg Granite Quarries (Felsenmeer)
description: Granite quarry in the Odenwald producing columns for Roman Trier. The 'sea of rocks' preserves abandoned Roman column drums.

name: Mayen Basalt Quarries
description: Basalt lava quarries in the Eifel producing millstones exported throughout the Roman northwest provinces.

name: Thracian Copper Mines (Burgas region)
description: Copper mining in Thrace, part of the Balkans' extensive mineral exploitation under Roman administration.

name: Lesbos Granite Quarries
description: Grey granite quarries on Lesbos, producing columns exported to Asia Minor and Rome.

name: Eastern Desert Gold Mines
description: Numerous gold mines in the Eastern Desert of Egypt, worked since pharaonic times and continued under Roman administration.

name: Mons Smaragdus (Sikait)
description: Emerald mines in the Eastern Desert of Roman Egypt, discovered under Ptolemaic rule and extensively worked under Rome.

name: Montemor-o-Novo Gold Mines
description: Gold extraction in the Alentejo region of Lusitania, with evidence of Roman-era hydraulic and underground mining.
